title: Designing a commonplace book, the Locke method
subtitle: How the traditional system of indexed note-taking works and how to set one up
excerpt: A commonplace book is a personal notebook organised around a topical index rather than a chronological sequence. The method described here follows John Locke's 1685 system, which groups entries by subject under a two-letter heading grid. It turns an ordinary notebook into a retrievable reference.
sourceNotes: John Locke, 'A New Method of Making Common-Place-Books' (written c.1685, first published in French 1686, English translation 1706): Locke's system uses a grid of initial letter and first vowel to produce a two-coordinate index without pagination at the start. His method was widely adopted in 18th-century England and is the basis of the system described here.

glossaryTerms.definition: A personal reference notebook in which extracts, observations, and notes are organised by topic rather than by date. The term derives from the Latin locus communis, a general heading under which material is collected.

A two-coordinate index system devised by John Locke (c.1685) in which entries are located by the first letter of the topic and the first vowel that follows it, producing a 20-cell grid that works for any notebook without requiring pre-numbered pages.

content.text: is not a diary and not a scrapbook. It is a working reference: a place to collect passages, ideas, and observations that can be found again. The system only works if the index is built from the start. A commonplace book started without an index is just a notebook.

occupies the first two pages of the notebook. Draw a grid of 20 cells: five columns (the vowels A, E, I, O, U) and four rows (groups of consonants, or the alphabet divided roughly evenly). Each cell holds the page numbers of entries whose topic begins with that letter combination. To find a note on 'paper', look under P+A. To add a new note, write it on the next free page and add that page number to the P+A cell.

Number every page before use, this is the one preparation step that cannot be done retroactively. Use a light pencil line and number each recto page; verso pages carry the same number with a verso mark if needed. Reserve the first two pages entirely for the index grid. All subsequent pages are content pages, filled in order of encounter rather than by topic.

Commonplace books have always mixed types: a passage from a book, a recipe, an address, a diagram. The only rule is that each entry has a topic heading in the margin or at the top of the section, so the index entry has something to point to. Cross-references between entries are encouraged, add secondary index entries in a different colour if the material spans more than one topic.

Any bound notebook works. A hardback with cream or ivory pages survives heavy use better than a perfect-bound softback. Larger page sizes accommodate diagrams and longer extracts; smaller formats are more portable. The Locke system was designed for quarto-sized books but scales to any size. If the notebook is handmade, allow for the index pages before sewing, leave the first two leaves loose for last-minute adjustments to the grid layout.
